B.1 Before coordinates, geometry and alg.prompt: Create a split historical tableau: on one side a geometer working with shapes, compass, and diagram; on the other an algebraist surrounded by symbolic manipulation. In the middle, leave an almost-empty bridge waiting to be built. The image should communicate that analytic geometry was a translation breakthrough between two already powerful but separate languages.
B.1 Before coordinates, geometry and alg.caption: Coordinates built a bridge between shape and symbol.

B.2 A point gets an address.role: Make coordinate assignment feel like choosing an address system, not discovering essence.
B.2 A point gets an address.prompt: Show a point in the plane receiving an address from two perpendicular reference directions, like a city intersection map translated into pure geometry. The point should remain the same object while different frames could, in principle, describe it differently. Clear, map-like, elegant, and adult.
B.2 A point gets an address.caption: A coordinate is an address relative to chosen axes.

B.3 Equation as locus, not just a senten.role: Shift equation-thinking from verbal command to geometric set of points.
B.3 Equation as locus, not just a senten.prompt: Illustrate an equation coming alive as a curve or line: countless points satisfying one common constraint and glowing into a visible locus. The image should make 'equation' feel like a habitat of points, not a line of text begging to be manipulated. Clean graph geometry with atmospheric restraint, ready for optional overlays.
B.3 Equation as locus, not just a senten.caption: An equation can describe a place, not only a puzzle.

B.4 Solving becomes intersection.role: Show simultaneous solution as where constraints meet.
B.4 Solving becomes intersection.prompt: Compose two curves or a curve and a line in the coordinate plane whose intersection point glows as the place where both constraints are true at once. The rest of each graph should remain visible enough that the meaning of simultaneous truth is felt. Elegant analytic geometry, uncluttered, overlay-friendly for exact graphs if needed.
B.4 Solving becomes intersection.caption: Solving can mean finding where two worlds agree.
B.4 Solving becomes intersection.overlay: Overlay exact axes and curve labels later.

B.5 Another lens: machine view and const.role: Offer two complementary readings of equations and functions.
B.5 Another lens: machine view and const.prompt: Design a split image. One side treats a formula as a machine transforming an input into an output. The other treats the same formula as a constraint carving out a shape or locus in space. The two views should visibly share structure rather than compete. Clean modern design, transparent layers, conceptual generosity.
B.5 Another lens: machine view and const.caption: The same expression can be read as a machine or as a constraint.

B.6 Historical note: analytic geometry c.role: Historicize the coordinate bridge as a true reorganization of the subject.
B.6 Historical note: analytic geometry c.prompt: Create a scene inspired by early modern analytic geometry: a room where diagrammatic geometry and symbolic algebra meet on the same surface, with curves gaining equations and equations gaining shape. Avoid portrait cliché; focus on the conceptual merger. Warm paper, precise lines, the feeling of a landscape being redrawn.
B.6 Historical note: analytic geometry c.caption: Once shape and symbol could translate, mathematics accelerated.

B.7 Why this bridge matters for the rest.role: Make coordinates feel like the hinge from geometry into functions and calculus.
B.7 Why this bridge matters for the rest.prompt: Design a bridge image in which a coordinate grid becomes a curve, the curve becomes a function, the function grows tangents and areas, and the whole analytic world of later chapters unfolds. The point is continuity: coordinates are not a side chapter but the gateway to modern curve-thinking. Spacious, elegant, future-facing.
B.7 Why this bridge matters for the rest.caption: Coordinates make later function and calculus thinking possible.

B.8 Thought experiments.role: Invite the learner to switch between shape and symbolic report.
B.8 Thought experiments.prompt: Show a point being read off a grid, a circle being recognized as the set of points a fixed distance from a center, and two crossing graphs turning into a solved system. Keep it sparse and portable, like a set of private check-your-understanding sketches. Mentally runnable at a glance.
B.8 Thought experiments.caption: Ask yourself whether you are reading an address, a machine, or a constraint.

14.1 The mapping itself is an object.role: Make the shift from action to object explicit and almost physical.
14.1 The mapping itself is an object.prompt: Illustrate a clear but elegant machine that takes in one kind of input and reliably produces one output, while the machine itself is highlighted as the real object of attention. Avoid childish gears; think transparent scientific instrument or sculptural mechanism. The reader should feel that a rule can itself be studied, combined, inverted, and transformed.
14.1 The mapping itself is an object.caption: A function is not just what it does; it is also a thing in its own right.

14.2 The graph makes the function visibl.role: Turn mapping into a visible curve in the plane.
14.2 The graph makes the function visibl.prompt: Show a set of input-output pairs rising into the plane and cohering into a continuous graph, with the curve felt as the visible body of the function. The image should treat the graph not as homework paper but as the function made visible. Elegant coordinate aesthetics, transparent grid, optional overlay for axes and points.
14.2 The graph makes the function visibl.caption: A graph is the function showing its body.
14.2 The graph makes the function visibl.overlay: Overlay axes and sample points later.

14.3 Composition is feeding one machine.role: Embodied composition as output flowing into the next input.
14.3 Composition is feeding one machine.prompt: Create a transparent two-stage machine or pipe system where the output of the first transformation literally feeds the input of the second, producing a final transformed result. The scene should make function composition feel practical and spatial. Clean, glass-like, adult, with a sense of process rather than clutter.
14.3 Composition is feeding one machine.caption: Composition means do one transformation, then feed its result into the next.

14.4 Inverse functions and reflection.role: Show inversion as input-output swapping and reflection across y=x.
14.4 Inverse functions and reflection.prompt: Illustrate a graph and its inverse as reflections across a luminous diagonal mirror line representing y equals x. The swap of input and output should be visible both as mirrored curve geometry and as a conceptual exchange of roles. Elegant and precise, with strong visual symmetry and room for optional annotation overlays.
14.4 Inverse functions and reflection.caption: Inverse functions are the same relation viewed with input and output exchanged.
14.4 Inverse functions and reflection.overlay: Overlay diagonal line and axes after generation.

14.5 Historical note: Euler and the rise.role: Place the abstraction of function in its historical emergence.
14.5 Historical note: Euler and the rise.prompt: Create a historical editorial scene where curves, formulas, and mappings begin to be treated as coherent objects in their own right. You can suggest Euler-era notation and paper culture without turning it into a portrait piece. The conceptual birth of function-thinking should dominate, not biographical trivia.
14.5 Historical note: Euler and the rise.caption: Functions became powerful when the mapping itself became an object of thought.

14.6 Thought experiments.role: Invite learners to think in mappings, graphs, and inverses.
14.6 Thought experiments.prompt: Show a tiny input-output machine, its graph in the plane, and a reflected inverse partner, all in one sparse triad. The point is to let the learner ask: what stays the same when I switch viewpoints? Minimal, clear, encouraging.
14.6 Thought experiments.caption: Move between machine view and graph view until they feel like the same thing.

15.1 A polynomial is a constructed trans.role: Make a polynomial feel assembled from shifts, scalings, and powers.
15.1 A polynomial is a constructed trans.prompt: Illustrate a machine assembled from modules labeled only by their visual action: a power-raising chamber, a scaling lever, a shift block, and an addition junction, all combining to generate one smooth output curve. The image should communicate that a polynomial is built from operations we already understand. Editorial-machine style, adult and precise.
15.1 A polynomial is a constructed trans.caption: A polynomial is a recipe built from familiar transformations.

15.2 Roots are where the machine outputs.role: Turn roots into crossings where the transformation hits the horizontal axis.
15.2 Roots are where the machine outputs.prompt: Show a polynomial curve crossing the x-axis at several points, with each crossing read as a place where the machine’s output vanishes. The axis should feel like a meaningful threshold, not just graph paper decoration. Elegant analytic illustration, optional overlay for curve specifics and factor hints.
15.2 Roots are where the machine outputs.caption: A root is where the transformation lands exactly on zero.
15.2 Roots are where the machine outputs.overlay: Overlay exact curves later if needed.

15.3 The dimension insight: abstract pol.role: Prevent dimensional nonsense by showing why raw lengths cannot usually be plugged into abstract polynomials unchanged.
15.3 The dimension insight: abstract pol.prompt: Create a split image where one side tries to add incompatible geometric species—a length term, an area term, a pure number term—and visibly fails to cohere, while the other side shows a clean dimensionless polynomial machine working on pure ratios. The image should feel like a powerful correction, not scolding. Transparent layers and type discipline are key.
15.3 The dimension insight: abstract pol.caption: Polynomials usually live in the world of pure ratios unless coefficients carry the missing dimensions.

15.4 Why polynomials matter so much.role: Show polynomials as the simplest smooth curves that approximate richer behavior.
15.4 Why polynomials matter so much.prompt: Design a bridge image in which simple polynomial curves act like scaffolding for more complicated smooth behaviors, gradually approximating richer functions. The composition should suggest that polynomials are the training ground and local language of calculus. Clean, elegant, future-leaning, with a sense of increasingly expressive smoothness.
15.4 Why polynomials matter so much.caption: Polynomials matter because they are the first smooth language rich enough to approximate much more.

15.5 Historical note: from Babylon to th.role: Give polynomial problem-solving a long civilizational arc.
15.5 Historical note: from Babylon to th.prompt: Create a restrained historical montage from ancient equation-solving on clay or parchment through Renaissance algebraic ambition into modern curve-based understanding. Avoid turning it into a jumble of names; focus on the enduring desire to solve structured symbolic problems and see their geometric consequences. Warm history meeting modern clarity.
15.5 Historical note: from Babylon to th.caption: Polynomial thinking has been part of the subject’s backbone for millennia.

15.6 Thought experiments.role: Invite the learner to imagine turning transformation knobs and watching axis crossings move.
15.6 Thought experiments.prompt: Show a simple curve with a few conceptual knobs for shifting up, stretching, or changing curvature, and watch its zero crossings slide. The sketch should feel like exploratory play for a serious mind, not gamification. Minimal, lucid, graph-like.
15.6 Thought experiments.caption: Change the machine slightly and watch where its output hits zero.

16.1 Calculus did not appear from nowher.role: Bridge all prior chapters into calculus as a natural culmination.
16.1 Calculus did not appear from nowher.prompt: Create a wide conceptual plate in which earlier motifs—ratio machine, nested intervals, slope as rate, accumulation by pieces, function curves—converge into a single calculus scene. The feeling should be inevitability, not abrupt novelty. This is the moment the reader realizes calculus is the same scaffold under a stronger microscope.
16.1 Calculus did not appear from nowher.caption: Calculus is not a new genre; it is the old scaffold under refinement.

16.2 Derivative: the slope that survives.role: Make the derivative feel like a stable answer emerging from repeated local refinement.
16.2 Derivative: the slope that survives.prompt: Illustrate a curve under progressive zoom, with secant lines from nearby points settling toward one tangent line that survives the magnification. The image should make the derivative feel discovered through stabilization, not defined by symbolic ritual. Precision optics vibe, elegant analytic geometry, optional overlay for curve and secants.
16.2 Derivative: the slope that survives.caption: The derivative is the slope that remains after enough honest zooming.
16.2 Derivative: the slope that survives.overlay: Overlay exact curve, point, and secant/tangent lines after generation.

16.3 Another lens: local linearity.role: Offer a gentler intuition: every smooth curve looks straighter up close.
16.3 Another lens: local linearity.prompt: Show a magnifying progression on a smooth curve where each closer view looks more and more like a straight line segment. The image should feel like a microscope discovering hidden linearity inside curvature. Clean, almost scientific-instrument aesthetic, but warm enough to reassure rather than intimidate.
16.3 Another lens: local linearity.caption: Local linearity is the visual secret behind derivatives.

16.4 Rates and units.role: Keep dimensional analysis alive inside derivative thinking.
16.4 Rates and units.prompt: Design a split or layered image where one derivative is displacement over time, another is area over length, and the output type changes accordingly. The visual emphasis should be that derivatives are rates with units, not abstract slopes floating free of context. Graphs, unit tags, and physical meaning should coexist without clutter.
16.4 Rates and units.caption: A derivative always says 'change in what, per change in what else?'

16.5 Integral: accumulation under refine.role: Make the integral feel like a stable total emerging from finer and finer pieces.
16.5 Integral: accumulation under refine.prompt: Illustrate area under a curve being approximated by rectangles that become thinner and more numerous until a smooth accumulated shape is trusted. The image should emphasize accumulation and refinement rather than rote symbols. Precision plus warmth; this is one of the book’s core conceptual images. Optional overlay for axes and partition lines.
16.5 Integral: accumulation under refine.caption: An integral is what survives when accumulated pieces are refined honestly.

16.6 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.role: Show derivative and integral as inverse-ish partners in one coherent visual loop.
16.6 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.prompt: Create a looped conceptual image in which a curve generates an accumulated-area function, and then the local rate of that accumulated function returns the original curve. Use a thin growing area strip and a tangent-rate motif linked in one elegant cycle. This should feel like one of the grand closure moments of the book—two opposite processes locking together.
16.6 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.caption: Accumulating rates and extracting rates are inverse partners.

16.7 Taylor series: local information re.role: Show increasingly rich local approximations reconstructing a function.
16.7 Taylor series: local information re.prompt: Illustrate a complicated smooth curve with a sequence of local polynomial approximations: constant, tangent line, parabola, then richer curves hugging the original more closely near a chosen point. The image should feel like zoomed local data being used to rebuild the wider behavior. Elegant layering, clear color hierarchy, no overload.
16.7 Taylor series: local information re.caption: Local information can rebuild astonishingly much of the whole.

16.8 Historical note: Newton, Leibniz, t.role: Place calculus inside both invention and later clarification.
16.8 Historical note: Newton, Leibniz, t.prompt: Create a disciplined historical montage: geometric tangent and area problems, then symbolic fluency with differentials, then later rigorous epsilon-limit refinement. The image should emphasize continuity more than rivalry. Think intellectual lineage, not duel narrative. Paper, diagrams, motion, measured modernity.
16.8 Historical note: Newton, Leibniz, t.caption: Calculus was invented powerfully before it was made fully rigorous.

16.9 Common confusions to avoid.role: Kill the usual confusions: derivative is not just 'the formula you get', integral is not just 'area sometimes'.
16.9 Common confusions to avoid.prompt: Design a corrective plate with mistaken textbook shortcuts on one side—derivative as dead algebra, integral as only shaded area—and the fuller structural meanings on the other: stable local rate and accumulated quantity under refinement. The right side should feel like a return of life to the subject. Sharp contrast, sophisticated editorial design.
16.9 Common confusions to avoid.caption: Do not reduce calculus to symbol manipulation divorced from the process it compresses.

16.10 Thought experiments.role: Invite the learner to run a small internal movie of zooming and accumulation.
16.10 Thought experiments.prompt: Show a point on a curve under a zoom lens becoming straight, and beside it a strip of rectangles getting finer under the same kind of refining gaze. Sparse, elegant, mentally runnable. The image should feel like a private meditation on what survives refinement.
16.10 Thought experiments.caption: Zoom in, refine the pieces, and trust the stable thing that remains.

17.1 Euclidean geometry already trained.role: Show special relativity as a change of invariant, not a break from all prior geometry.
17.1 Euclidean geometry already trained.prompt: Create a bridge image that starts with the familiar Euclidean distance invariant in a spatial grid and then morphs into a spacetime grid where a different quadratic form is preserved. The viewer should feel continuity: this is still geometry, but the preserved quantity has changed. Austere, elegant, deeply structured.
17.1 Euclidean geometry already trained.caption: Relativity is strange, but it is still geometry organized by invariants.

17.2 Relativity changes the symmetry gro.role: Make the change from rotations to Lorentz transformations visually graspable.
17.2 Relativity changes the symmetry gro.prompt: Illustrate two kinds of preserved structures side by side: Euclidean circles under rotations and hyperbola-like invariant structures under Lorentz-style spacetime transformations. The scene should feel like the rulebook of symmetry has changed while the game of preserving structure remains the same. Minimal, modernist, high clarity.
17.2 Relativity changes the symmetry gro.caption: The crucial change is which transformations preserve the world’s law.

17.3 Energy and momentum form a spacetim.role: Make the energy-momentum relation feel like another invariant vector story.
17.3 Energy and momentum form a spacetim.prompt: Create a modernist plate where energy and momentum components assemble into a four-dimensional vector-like object, and an invariant relation glows as the preserved 'length' of that object. You need not literalize all four dimensions; instead, use layered projections and a Minkowski-style geometry cue. Precise, restrained, powerful. Optional overlay for formulas later.
17.3 Energy and momentum form a spacetim.caption: Energy and momentum belong together as one invariant structure.

17.4 Why this belongs in the same book.role: Show the continuity from earlier invariants to modern physics.
17.4 Why this belongs in the same book.prompt: Design a visual family portrait of invariants across the book: translation differences, rotational distances, scaling ratios, complex magnitudes, spacetime intervals, and energy-momentum invariants all arranged as descendants of one shared idea. The image should be explanatory, not merely decorative. The viewer should feel that relativity is a late chapter in the same long story.
17.4 Why this belongs in the same book.caption: The invariant becomes the law, whether in geometry or physics.

17.5 Historical note: Einstein, Minkowsk.role: Historicize the move from physical principle to geometric language.
17.5 Historical note: Einstein, Minkowsk.prompt: Create a historically sensitive editorial scene where messy measurements and physical principles are re-expressed through a cleaner spacetime geometry. The image may hint at Einstein’s physical insight and Minkowski’s geometric recasting without turning into a biographical poster. Modern paper, chalkboards, light cones, disciplined restraint.
17.5 Historical note: Einstein, Minkowsk.caption: The geometric recasting of physics was an upgrade in language, not mere ornament.

17.6 A brief modern echo: Noether.role: Tie invariance to conservation law in a single memorable image.
17.6 A brief modern echo: Noether.prompt: Illustrate a modern, clean conceptual scene in which a symmetry operation leaves an action-like system unchanged and, in a linked chamber, a conserved quantity remains steady. The relation between symmetry and conservation should feel almost magical but structurally crisp. Noether should appear as an intellectual presence, not a portrait requirement. Elegant, compressed, memorable.
17.6 A brief modern echo: Noether.caption: Symmetry is not decoration; it generates conservation.

17.7 Thought experiments.role: Invite intuitive rehearsal of invariant change under changed frames.
17.7 Thought experiments.prompt: Show two observers slicing the same spacetime event pattern differently while one interval remains visually unchanged, alongside a tiny echo of a rotated Euclidean vector whose length stays fixed. Sparse and conceptual, like a notebook experiment comparing two kinds of invariance. Calm, modern, lucid.
17.7 Thought experiments.caption: Change the frame and watch what changes; then notice what stubbornly does not.

18.1 Build the real numbers rigorously.role: Make the next step feel like reinforcing a bridge pier, not restarting from scratch.
18.1 Build the real numbers rigorously.prompt: Illustrate the real number line under construction at a deeper level: nested intervals, Dedekind cuts, and Cauchy-like convergence as reinforcing beams under an already-walkable bridge. The image should say that rigor is reinforcement of intuition, not hostility to intuition. Architectural, calm, confidence-building.
18.1 Build the real numbers rigorously.caption: Rigor is reinforcement, not betrayal.

18.2 Re-derive algebraic laws from the g.role: Frame proof of laws as paying down conceptual debt.
18.2 Re-derive algebraic laws from the g.prompt: Show familiar algebraic laws etched on translucent sheets hovering above the geometric constructions that generate them—distributivity from scaling joined segments, fraction rules from inverse scaling, sign rules from symmetries. The image should feel like debts being settled and slogans earning their keep. Elegant, scholarly, not overloaded.
18.2 Re-derive algebraic laws from the g.caption: A law becomes trustworthy when you can see what geometry forced it.

18.3 Go deeper on Euler\u2019s formula a.role: Present deeper study as thickening one of the most beautiful bridges in the book.
18.3 Go deeper on Euler\u2019s formula a.prompt: Create a richer version of the Euler bridge where complex exponentials, rotations, waves, and differential equations start to braid together. This should look like an invitation to a deeper chamber, not a rehash. Luminous, modernist, mathematically romantic without becoming cheesy.
18.3 Go deeper on Euler\u2019s formula a.caption: This is one bridge worth walking farther than once.

18.4 Differential equations: laws of cha.role: Show the next frontier after derivative-as-rate: whole families of curves obeying local laws.
18.4 Differential equations: laws of cha.prompt: Illustrate a slope field or directional texture in the plane giving rise to many possible solution curves that flow through it. The image should make the subject feel like geometry of laws rather than symbol warfare. Calm, organic, precise, future-facing.
18.4 Differential equations: laws of cha.caption: Differential equations ask which curves obey a local law everywhere.

18.5 Linear algebra as the grown-up form.role: Show later study as deepening, not abandoning, the transformation viewpoint.
18.5 Linear algebra as the grown-up form.prompt: Create an image where simple arrows and grids from earlier chapters mature into richer linear-algebra structures: eigen-directions, matrix actions, coordinated transformations of space. The tone should be 'same story, larger room.' Clean engineering elegance, strong continuity with section 13.
18.5 Linear algebra as the grown-up form.caption: Linear algebra is transformation geometry with more serious muscles.

18.6 Probability and statistics as struc.role: Open a door from deterministic geometry into distributions, inference, and information.
18.6 Probability and statistics as struc.prompt: Show a cloud of data points, a smooth probability curve, and an information-like compression motif sharing one clean space. The image should avoid casino clichés completely. The point is that uncertainty can be structured, quantified, and reasoned about as carefully as geometry. Modern, calm, intellectually inviting.
18.6 Probability and statistics as struc.caption: Probability is not the enemy of structure; it is structure under uncertainty.

18.7 Topology and geometry beyond rigid.role: Invite curiosity about new sameness relations beyond congruence and similarity.
18.7 Topology and geometry beyond rigid.prompt: Illustrate a shape smoothly deforming into another while preserving connectivity, alongside earlier rigid or similarity-preserving scenes fading in the background. The image should make topology feel like a new answer to the old question 'what counts as the same?' Elegant, tangible, not rubber-duck cartoon topology.
18.7 Topology and geometry beyond rigid.caption: New mathematics often begins by changing the notion of sameness.

19.1 Abstract algebra: symmetry becomes.role: Show symmetry patterns detached from any single geometric carrier.
19.1 Abstract algebra: symmetry becomes.prompt: Create a conceptual scene where concrete symmetries of shapes, equations, and transformations are distilled into a shared abstract pattern—like a crystalline network or operation table without text. The point is that the structure of composition itself has become the thing being studied. Modern, abstract, crisp, not decorative.
19.1 Abstract algebra: symmetry becomes.caption: Abstract algebra studies the structure of possible symmetries and compositions.

19.2 Topology: sameness under continuous.role: Make topological sameness feel like a new, gentler equivalence relation.
19.2 Topology: sameness under continuous.prompt: Show one shape slowly deforming into another without tearing or gluing, with continuity of the transformation foregrounded. The image should feel serious and elegant rather than novelty-driven. Use tangible surfaces and continuous motion traces so the learner feels what is preserved when rigid shape is no longer sacred.
19.2 Topology: sameness under continuous.caption: Topology changes the answer to 'what counts as the same?'

19.3 Differential geometry: calculus on.role: Show curvature, local flatness, and calculus living together.
19.3 Differential geometry: calculus on.prompt: Illustrate a smooth curved surface with a tiny tangent plane attached locally, and perhaps a path or field flowing across it. The image should make clear that locally the space can still look linear enough for calculus, even while globally it bends. Clean, luminous, concept-driven, quietly beautiful.
19.3 Differential geometry: calculus on.caption: Differential geometry asks calculus to live on curved worlds.

19.4 Analysis: rigor about limit, approx.role: Show analysis as the disciplined study of the refinement patterns already present throughout the book.
19.4 Analysis: rigor about limit, approx.prompt: Create a spare conceptual image composed of nested intervals, convergent sequences, approximation layers, and continuity of curves, all gathered under one austere visual language of precision. The point is not symbol overload but disciplined limit-thinking as a whole domain. Elegant and almost monastic.
19.4 Analysis: rigor about limit, approx.caption: Analysis is the culture of taking approximation and limit seriously.

19.5 Probability, information, and compu.role: Connect modern mathematical thinking to uncertainty, encoding, and algorithmic process.
19.5 Probability, information, and compu.prompt: Design a contemporary image where probability clouds, encoded bit-like structures, and algorithmic flow diagrams share one coherent space. Avoid tech-bro cliché and glowing nonsense. The visual should suggest that modern mathematics studies pattern, uncertainty, and computation together in deep ways. Crisp, contemporary, intelligent.
19.5 Probability, information, and compu.caption: Modern mathematics also lives in information, uncertainty, and process.

19.6 Why this modern map matters for a r.role: Emotionally reframe advanced math as a navigable atlas, not a wall of alien jargon.
19.6 Why this modern map matters for a r.prompt: Create a compassionate but serious image of a person or implied viewpoint standing before a richly connected map of mathematical territories—algebra, topology, geometry, analysis, probability—where paths and bridges are visible instead of a forbidding cliff. The tone should be dignifying and invitational, never childish or self-help-ish. This image is about restoring agency.
19.6 Why this modern map matters for a r.caption: Advanced mathematics is a landscape you can enter, not a wall built to exclude you.

19.7 Final thesis.role: Conclude with the book’s deepest claim: mathematics is organized by objects, transformations, and invariants across every scale.
19.7 Final thesis.prompt: Create a culminating synthesis image that gathers the book’s recurring motifs—segment, arrow, dilation rays, circle, graph, tangent, complex plane, spacetime grid—into one coherent constellation. The center of the composition should be the idea of invariance under transformation, with every chapter’s object orbiting it. Grand but disciplined, intellectually beautiful, emotionally earned.
19.7 Final thesis.caption: Across the whole book, the invariant under the right transformation becomes the real thing to understand.

Cover / title page.role: Set the tone for the whole book: serious, beautiful, first-principles, transformation-centered, adult, and visually irresistible without becoming sci-fi wallpaper.
Cover / title page.prompt: Create a cover illustration for an adult mathematics book that begins with a single segment and unfolds through arrow, circle, graph, complex plane, and spacetime grid into a coherent transformation-based cosmos. The composition should feel like mathematics growing from concrete geometry into modern abstraction without ever breaking its spine. Rich but disciplined, elegant typography space at top and center, no embedded text, no classroom cliché, no random equations floating in fog.
Cover / title page.caption: From segment to spacetime, one scaffold.

Front matter opener: school made mathema.role: Acknowledge the reader’s experience without pity and signal that this book rebuilds trust.
Front matter opener: school made mathema.prompt: Show a sterile worksheet-like world of disconnected symbols fading away while, in the foreground, real geometric objects and transformations—sticks, arrows, circles, curves—re-enter as tangible, intelligible things. The mood should be relief and re-entry into meaning, not therapy cliché. Adult, dignified, low-drama, intellectually welcoming.
Front matter opener: school made mathema.caption: This book returns mathematics to things you can see and act on.

Decoding tool opener: picture, action, r.role: Give the reader the four-lens method in one memorable image.
Decoding tool opener: picture, action, r.prompt: Design a four-quadrant image around one mathematical idea—such as addition or derivative—shown as picture, action, relation, and compression. Each quadrant should feel different in medium but clearly belong to the same concept: a drawn scene, a transformation process, an invariant relationship, and a symbolic packaging. Elegant, didactic without being textbooky, overlay-friendly for labels later.
Decoding tool opener: picture, action, r.caption: Every new idea can be decoded four ways.

Permanent scaffold plate.role: Give the whole document a recurring visual anchor that readers can return to.
Permanent scaffold plate.prompt: Create a master visual atlas linking objects, sameness relations, operations, and invariants across the whole book. Segments, directed arrows, triangles, curves, functions, vectors, and spacetime intervals should appear as stations in one transformation-based system. The image should be rich enough to revisit throughout the book but not crowded. Elegant editorial map, adult palette, room for later labels.
Permanent scaffold plate.caption: Objects, operations, symmetries, invariants: the whole spine at once.

Roadmap of the journey.role: Make the five-part structure feel like a navigable expedition rather than a syllabus.
Roadmap of the journey.prompt: Design a five-part journey map that begins with raw magnitudes and ends in modern mathematical landscapes. Each part should have its own terrain character—workshop, instrument room, celestial chamber, analytic city, spacetime atlas—while remaining connected by a single path of transformations and invariants. The tone should be exploratory and rigorous, not fantasy-cartoon.
Roadmap of the journey.caption: This is a journey through one evolving language, not a pile of disconnected units.

Part I opener.role: Set the tactile, pre-symbolic tone of the first part.
Part I opener.prompt: Create a tactile opening image for Part I: wood, rope, brass compass, straight segments, and unlabeled geometric comparisons laid out like the first honest tools of mathematics before symbolism takes over. The mood should be grounded, physical, and quietly profound.
Part I opener.caption: Before symbols, there were magnitudes, comparisons, and choices.

Part II opener.role: Announce operations as transformations rather than arithmetic recipes.
Part II opener.prompt: Create a Part II opener in which arrows, scaling rays, mirrors, and ratio machines appear as a family of operations acting on a common geometric world. The image should feel like entering a room full of instruments that do mathematics by moving, resizing, reversing, and composing. Elegant, kinetic, no clutter.
Part II opener.caption: Arithmetic becomes transformation.

Part III opener.role: Signal expansion beyond the rational line into turning, completeness, and the plane.
Part III opener.prompt: Create a Part III opener where a number line opens into nested intervals, a turning circle, and finally the complex plane under a night-sky or observatory mood. The feeling should be expansion of stage and depth, not abandonment of prior structure. Luminous, precise, adult.
Part III opener.caption: The line widens into circle, plane, and completeness.

Part IV opener.role: Set the mood for coordinates, functions, and calculus as curve-thinking.
Part IV opener.prompt: Create a Part IV opener where coordinate grids, curves, machines, tangents, and accumulations flow together like a transparent city of analytic structures. It should feel modern, lucid, and full of motion under refinement. Crisp glassy layers, elegant geometry, no software-dashboard vibe.
Part IV opener.caption: Curves become objects; change becomes visible.

Part V opener.role: Elevate the final part into an invariants-and-modernity chamber.
Part V opener.prompt: Create a Part V opener where Euclidean geometry, complex rotations, and spacetime invariants line up as one family of preserved structures under different symmetry groups. The image should feel austere, modern, and culminating. Black, silver, deep blue, strong structure, no sci-fi noise.
Part V opener.caption: At the end of the road, the invariant becomes the law.

Appendix A timeline.role: Give the historical timeline a clean visual spine so it feels like living development rather than trivia.
Appendix A timeline.prompt: Design a horizontally flowing timeline mural that moves from ancient geometry and ratio crises through analytic geometry, calculus, complex numbers, linear algebra, relativity, and modern branches. Use a restrained icon system and evolving visual motifs rather than portraits alone. Clean, elegant, highly navigable.
Appendix A timeline.caption: The subject grew by answering pressure, not by accreting random facts.

Appendix D proof-reading guide.role: Turn proof-reading from dread into a navigable practice.
Appendix D proof-reading guide.prompt: Create an image of a proof as a path through a structured landscape: assumptions at one end, allowed moves as stepping stones, conclusions at the far side, and common wrong turns faintly visible but not dominant. The image should be calming and procedural without becoming childish. It should tell the reader that a proof is a route to be walked, not a wall to be feared.
Appendix D proof-reading guide.caption: A proof is a path of allowed moves, not a performance of brilliance.
